question: Define an isolated point of a subset S in a metric space (X, d). Provide a precise definition and a simple illustrative example.
expectedPoints: A point x ∈ S is an isolated point of S if there exists an ε > 0 such that the open ball B(x, ε) contains no other points of S.
The definition must correctly use the metric d to define the open ball.
A valid example, such as the point 1 in the subset S = {1} ∪ [2, 3] of R with the standard metric, explaining why it is isolated.

question: Explain the relationship between limit points and isolated points. Prove that a point in a subset S of a metric space cannot be both a limit point and an isolated point of S.
expectedPoints: A limit point of S is a point x (which may or may not be in S) such that every neighborhood of x contains a point of S different from x.
An isolated point is a point in S that has a neighborhood containing no other points of S.

Proof by contradiction: Assume a point x ∈ S is both. Then there exists an ε1 > 0 such that B(x, ε1) ∩ S = {x} (from isolated definition). But for x to be a limit point, for any ε > 0 (e.g., ε = ε1), B(x, ε) must contain a point of S different from x. This is a contradiction.

question: Consider the set S = {1/n : n ∈ ℕ} as a subset of ℝ with the standard Euclidean metric. Identify all limit points and all isolated points of S. Justify your answers rigorously by applying the definitions and considering the topology of the space.
expectedPoints: Identification of the isolated points: Every point 1/n is isolated. For each specific 1/n, choose ε such that 1/(n+1) < 1/n - ε (e.g., ε = (1/n - 1/(n+1))/2). The ball B(1/n, ε) will contain no other points of S.

Identification of the limit point: 0 is a limit point of S. For any ε > 0, by the Archimedean property, there exists an n such that 1/n < ε, so B(0, ε) contains the point 1/n from S (which is different from 0).

Proof that no other limit points exist: For any x ≠ 0 and x not in S, or for any x in S (which is an isolated point), one can find an ε-neighborhood that does not contain other points of S (or any points of S if x is not in S).
